title: Static Accessibility Heuristics, Semantics, and Human Review · Contract Defense
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for static accessibility heuristics, semantics, and human review and accepts only compiler plus runtime changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named network, DNS, browser, accessibility, security, load, legal-review, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for WCAG-linked static audit with explicit limits. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case…
objectives: Classify informative, decorative, functional, linked, complex, grouped, and text images using context and route uncertain cases to review.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Conformance Nonclaim
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 5085 during an isolated browser, package, and restore smoke. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to map static image, structure, language, semantics, and interaction risks to WCAG with review handoff; every external value remains unknown until runtime admission produces an owned value; then change one duplicate, accessibility state, axe result, resource budget, browser readiness, or manual-review condition without overclaiming. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Map findings to WCAG 2.2 criteria, page states, severity rationale, affected users, and human verification without claiming automated conformance.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Conformance Nonclaim
steps.instruction:
Write the quiz evidence note for Conformance Nonclaim: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 5276 during an origin-fairness and event-loop capacity rehearsal. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to map static image, structure, language, semantics, and interaction risks to WCAG with review handoff; owner authorization and revocation must remain separate from robots and legal-review claims; then change one owner permission, seed, URL, robots, sitemap, redirect, DNS, media, or encoding decision and reconcile provenance. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Map findings to WCAG 2.2 criteria, page states, severity rationale, affected users, and human verification without claiming automated conformance.

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Conformance Nonclaim.
steps.why: Retrieving Conformance Nonclaim through the concrete WCAG-linked static audit with explicit limits case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Conformance Nonclaim, keep Conformance Nonclaim connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Map findings to WCAG 2.2 criteria, page states, severity rationale, affected users, and human verification without claiming automated conformance.
